texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Temporal structure and gain/loss asymmetry for real and artificial stock indices
texts.text: Temporal structure and gain/loss asymmetry for real and artificial stock indices

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Johannes Vitalis Siven a , Jeffrey Todd Lins a
texts.text: Johannes Vitalis Siven a , Jeffrey Todd Lins a

texts.label: text
texts.orig: a Saxo Bank A/S, Philip Heymans All´ e 15, DK-2900 Hellerup, Denmark
texts.text: a Saxo Bank A/S, Philip Heymans All´ e 15, DK-2900 Hellerup, Denmark

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We demonstrate that the gain/loss asymmetry observed for stock indices vanishes if the temporal dependence structure is destroyed by scrambling the time series. We also show that an artificial index constructed by a simple average of a number of individual stocks display gain/loss asymmetry - this allows us to explicitly analyze the dependence between the index constituents. We consider mutual information and correlation based measures and show that the stock returns indeed have a higher degree of dependence in times of market downturns than upturns.
texts.text: We demonstrate that the gain/loss asymmetry observed for stock indices vanishes if the temporal dependence structure is destroyed by scrambling the time series. We also show that an artificial index constructed by a simple average of a number of individual stocks display gain/loss asymmetry - this allows us to explicitly analyze the dependence between the index constituents. We consider mutual information and correlation based measures and show that the stock returns indeed have a higher degree of dependence in times of market downturns than upturns.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Key words: Temporal structure, gain/loss asymmetry, mutual information
texts.text: Key words: Temporal structure, gain/loss asymmetry, mutual information

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Introduction
texts.text: Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Inspired by research in the field of turbulence, Simonsen, Jensen, and Johansen [1] considered 'inverse statistics' of financial time series: what is the smallest time interval needed for an asset to cross a fixed return level ρ ? Figure 1 shows the distribution of this random variable, the fi rst passage time , for the Dow Jones Industrial Average index, for ρ = ± 5%. As noted by Jensen, Johansen, and Simonsen [2], the most likely first passage time is shorter for ρ = -5% than for ρ = 5%, which they refer to as the gain/loss asymmetry .
texts.text: Inspired by research in the field of turbulence, Simonsen, Jensen, and Johansen [1] considered 'inverse statistics' of financial time series: what is the smallest time interval needed for an asset to cross a fixed return level ρ ? Figure 1 shows the distribution of this random variable, the fi rst passage time , for the Dow Jones Industrial Average index, for ρ = ± 5%. As noted by Jensen, Johansen, and Simonsen [2], the most likely first passage time is shorter for ρ = -5% than for ρ = 5%, which they refer to as the gain/loss asymmetry .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we show that the gain/loss asymmetry in the Dow Jones index vanishes if the time series is 'scrambled' - that is, if one considers a new time series constructed by randomly permuting the returns. This basic fact, which seems to have gone unnoticed in the literature so far, has important implications: the gain/loss asymmetry is not due to properties of the unconditional index returns, like skewness, but is rather an expression of potentially complex temporal structure. This finding resonates with the results from Siven, Lins, and Lundbek Hansen [3], where wavelet analysis is used to demonstrate that the gain/loss asymmetry is a long time scale phenomenon - it vanishes if enough low frequency content is removed from the index, that is, if the index is sufficiently 'detrended.' Siven, Lins, and Lundbek Hansen [3] also present a generalization of the asymmetric synchronous market model from Donangelo, Jensen, Simonsen, and Sneppen [4] where prolonged periods of high correlation between the individual stocks during index downturns gives rise to a gain/loss asymmetry.
texts.text: In this paper, we show that the gain/loss asymmetry in the Dow Jones index vanishes if the time series is 'scrambled' - that is, if one considers a new time series constructed by randomly permuting the returns. This basic fact, which seems to have gone unnoticed in the literature so far, has important implications: the gain/loss asymmetry is not due to properties of the unconditional index returns, like skewness, but is rather an expression of potentially complex temporal structure. This finding resonates with the results from Siven, Lins, and Lundbek Hansen [3], where wavelet analysis is used to demonstrate that the gain/loss asymmetry is a long time scale phenomenon - it vanishes if enough low frequency content is removed from the index, that is, if the index is sufficiently 'detrended.' Siven, Lins, and Lundbek Hansen [3] also present a generalization of the asymmetric synchronous market model from Donangelo, Jensen, Simonsen, and Sneppen [4] where prolonged periods of high correlation between the individual stocks during index downturns gives rise to a gain/loss asymmetry.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Whether the constituents of e.g. the Dow Jones index indeed tend to move with a greater degree of dependence during market downturns could in principle be tested empirically by analysis of the time series of the individual stocks. That is an awkward task, however, since the relative weights for different stocks in these indices have changed over time in complicated ways. To address this issue, we demonstrate that if one defines a new, artificial index by simply taking the average of a number of stocks, this index also displays gain/loss asymmetry. With the constituents readily available, we consider two measures based on correlation and mutual information, and show that there indeed is a higher degree of dependence between the stock returns during index downturns than upturns.
texts.text: Whether the constituents of e.g. the Dow Jones index indeed tend to move with a greater degree of dependence during market downturns could in principle be tested empirically by analysis of the time series of the individual stocks. That is an awkward task, however, since the relative weights for different stocks in these indices have changed over time in complicated ways. To address this issue, we demonstrate that if one defines a new, artificial index by simply taking the average of a number of stocks, this index also displays gain/loss asymmetry. With the constituents readily available, we consider two measures based on correlation and mutual information, and show that there indeed is a higher degree of dependence between the stock returns during index downturns than upturns.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Gain/loss asymmetry and temporal structure
texts.text: Gain/loss asymmetry and temporal structure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For a given process { I t } t ≥ 0 , for instance daily closing prices of a stock index, the first passage time τ ρ of the level ρ is defined as
texts.text: For a given process { I t } t ≥ 0 , for instance daily closing prices of a stock index, the first passage time τ ρ of the level ρ is defined as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: τ ρ = { inf { s > 0; log( I t + s /I t ) ≥ ρ } if ρ > 0 , inf { s > 0; log( I t + s /I t ) ≤ ρ } if ρ < 0 ,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: and is assumed to be independent of t . The distribution of τ ρ is estimated in a straightforward manner from a time series I 0 , . . . , I T . Consider ρ > 0, and let t + s be the smallest time point such that log( I t + s /I t ) ≥ ρ , if such a time point exists. In that case, s is viewed as an observation of τ ρ . (If ρ < 0, take instead t + s such that log( I t + s /I t ) ≤ ρ .) Running t from 0 to T -1 gives a set of observations from which the distribution of τ ρ is estimated as the empirical distribution. Given the empirical distribution, we follow Jensen, Johansen, and Simonsen [2] and compute a fit of the density function for the generalized gamma distribution. This density is plotted as a solid line together with the empirical distribution in all figures, to guide the eye - we do not discuss the fitted parameters, nor claim that τ ρ truly follows a generalized gamma distribution.
texts.text: and is assumed to be independent of t . The distribution of τ ρ is estimated in a straightforward manner from a time series I 0 , . . . , I T . Consider ρ > 0, and let t + s be the smallest time point such that log( I t + s /I t ) ≥ ρ , if such a time point exists. In that case, s is viewed as an observation of τ ρ . (If ρ < 0, take instead t + s such that log( I t + s /I t ) ≤ ρ .) Running t from 0 to T -1 gives a set of observations from which the distribution of τ ρ is estimated as the empirical distribution. Given the empirical distribution, we follow Jensen, Johansen, and Simonsen [2] and compute a fit of the density function for the generalized gamma distribution. This density is plotted as a solid line together with the empirical distribution in all figures, to guide the eye - we do not discuss the fitted parameters, nor claim that τ ρ truly follows a generalized gamma distribution.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Gain/loss asymmetry for the Dow Jones index
texts.text: Gain/loss asymmetry for the Dow Jones index

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 1 shows the estimated first passage time for the Dow Jones index. As discussed in the Introduction, there is a gain/loss asymmetry in that the most likely first passage time is shorter for ρ = -5% than for ρ = 5%. Next, we construct a scrambled version of the index by randomly re-arranging the log returns. Formally, if I 0 , . . . , I T denotes the time series of daily closing prices of the Dow Jones index, let δI t = log( I t /I t -1 ) for t = 1 , . . . , T and draw a random permutation j 1 , . . . , j T of { 1 , . . . , T } . We define
texts.text: Figure 1 shows the estimated first passage time for the Dow Jones index. As discussed in the Introduction, there is a gain/loss asymmetry in that the most likely first passage time is shorter for ρ = -5% than for ρ = 5%. Next, we construct a scrambled version of the index by randomly re-arranging the log returns. Formally, if I 0 , . . . , I T denotes the time series of daily closing prices of the Dow Jones index, let δI t = log( I t /I t -1 ) for t = 1 , . . . , T and draw a random permutation j 1 , . . . , j T of { 1 , . . . , T } . We define

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: δ ˜ I t = δI j t , for t = 1 , . . . , T ,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: and let the scrambled index be given by ˜ I 0 = I 0 and
texts.text: and let the scrambled index be given by ˜ I 0 = I 0 and

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: ˜ I t = ˜ I 0 exp ( t ∑ s =1 δ ˜ I s ) , for t = 1 , . . . , T .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 1 shows that the scrambled index does not display a gain/loss asymmetry. This result is surprisingly strong: since the empirical return distributions are identical for an index and any of its scrambled versions, it shows that the gain/loss asymmetry is an expression of potentially complex temporal structure in the index. This fits nicely with the results from Siven, Lins, and Lundbek Hansen [3], where a multiscale decomposition is used to demonstrate that the gain/loss asymmetry is a long rather than short scale phenomenon.
texts.text: Figure 1 shows that the scrambled index does not display a gain/loss asymmetry. This result is surprisingly strong: since the empirical return distributions are identical for an index and any of its scrambled versions, it shows that the gain/loss asymmetry is an expression of potentially complex temporal structure in the index. This fits nicely with the results from Siven, Lins, and Lundbek Hansen [3], where a multiscale decomposition is used to demonstrate that the gain/loss asymmetry is a long rather than short scale phenomenon.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gain/loss asymmetry in the asymmetric synchronous market model from Donangelo, Jensen, Simonsen, and Sneppen [4] does not disappear when the index returns are scrambled. This is to be expected, since the daily returns in that model are independent and identically distributed, so all statistical properties remain the same when the time series is scrambled. However, for the generalized model proposed in Siven, Lins, and Lundbek Hansen [3] the asymmetry does vanish, in perfect agreement with the Dow Jones index, see Figure 2.
texts.text: The gain/loss asymmetry in the asymmetric synchronous market model from Donangelo, Jensen, Simonsen, and Sneppen [4] does not disappear when the index returns are scrambled. This is to be expected, since the daily returns in that model are independent and identically distributed, so all statistical properties remain the same when the time series is scrambled. However, for the generalized model proposed in Siven, Lins, and Lundbek Hansen [3] the asymmetry does vanish, in perfect agreement with the Dow Jones index, see Figure 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We consider historical stock prices from January 1970 until December 2008 for the following 12 Dow Jones constituents: Boeing Co. (BA), Citigroup Inc. (C), El DuPont de Nemours & Co. (DD), General Electric Co. (GE), General Motors Corporation (GM), International business Machines Corp. (IBM), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), The Coca-Cola Company (KO), McDonald's Corp. (MCD), Procter & Gamble Co. (PG), and Alcoa Inc. (AA). These companies are chosen since long time series of stock returns are available, but our results are stable in the sense that adding or removing companies give very similar results.
texts.text: We consider historical stock prices from January 1970 until December 2008 for the following 12 Dow Jones constituents: Boeing Co. (BA), Citigroup Inc. (C), El DuPont de Nemours & Co. (DD), General Electric Co. (GE), General Motors Corporation (GM), International business Machines Corp. (IBM), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), The Coca-Cola Company (KO), McDonald's Corp. (MCD), Procter & Gamble Co. (PG), and Alcoa Inc. (AA). These companies are chosen since long time series of stock returns are available, but our results are stable in the sense that adding or removing companies give very similar results.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 3 shows that the index constructed from these stocks display a gain/loss asymmetry, much like the Dow Jones index, and that the asymmetry vanishes if we scramble the time series.
texts.text: Figure 3 shows that the index constructed from these stocks display a gain/loss asymmetry, much like the Dow Jones index, and that the asymmetry vanishes if we scramble the time series.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In what follows, we will use this artificial index as a kind of proxy for a real stock index. This has the advantage that the individual index constituents are readily available for analysis. This is unlike the Dow Jones index for which the relative weights and indeed the set of constituents have changed over time.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Dependence between constituents during periods of index upturns and downturns
texts.text: Dependence between constituents during periods of index upturns and downturns

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Here, and in what follows, { I t } t =0 ,...,T denotes the artificial index defined in the previous section.
texts.text: Here, and in what follows, { I t } t =0 ,...,T denotes the artificial index defined in the previous section.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Inspired by the generalized asymmetric synchronous market model from Siven, Lins, and Lundbek Hansen [3], our general intuition is that the individual stocks tend to 'move together' to a greater degree during index downturns than during upturns, resulting in more violent downturns than upturns. To quantify this, we first divide the price history of our artificial index I into two parts, corresponding to upturns and downturns, respectively.
texts.text: Inspired by the generalized asymmetric synchronous market model from Siven, Lins, and Lundbek Hansen [3], our general intuition is that the individual stocks tend to 'move together' to a greater degree during index downturns than during upturns, resulting in more violent downturns than upturns. To quantify this, we first divide the price history of our artificial index I into two parts, corresponding to upturns and downturns, respectively.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Mean mutual information
texts.text: Mean mutual information

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The mutual information of two discrete stochastic variables X and Y is defined as
texts.text: The mutual information of two discrete stochastic variables X and Y is defined as

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: M ( X,Y ) = ∑ x ∑ y p XY ( x, y ) log ( p XY ( x, y ) p X ( x ) p Y ( y ) ) ,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where p XY denote the joint and p X and p Y the marginal probability functions of X and Y . Mutual information can be written as M ( X,Y ) = H ( X )+ H ( Y ) -H ( X,Y ), where H ( X ) and H ( Y ) are the marginal entropies, and H ( X,Y ) is the joint entropy of X and Y , and it is a measure of dependence in the sense that X and Y are independent if and only if M ( X,Y ) = 0. Mutual information can estimated from a finite set { ( X t , Y t ) } t =1 ,...,n of joint samples of ( X,Y ) in a number of different ways, see Paninski [5]. In the computations below we apply the most straightforward estimator, the so-called plug-in estimator.
texts.text: where p XY denote the joint and p X and p Y the marginal probability functions of X and Y . Mutual information can be written as M ( X,Y ) = H ( X )+ H ( Y ) -H ( X,Y ), where H ( X ) and H ( Y ) are the marginal entropies, and H ( X,Y ) is the joint entropy of X and Y , and it is a measure of dependence in the sense that X and Y are independent if and only if M ( X,Y ) = 0. Mutual information can estimated from a finite set { ( X t , Y t ) } t =1 ,...,n of joint samples of ( X,Y ) in a number of different ways, see Paninski [5]. In the computations below we apply the most straightforward estimator, the so-called plug-in estimator.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let M U,n and M D,n denote the mutual information of the returns of the n th stock and index, estimated from the samples { ( δS n,t , δI n,t ) } t ∈ U respectively { ( δS n,t , δI n,t ) } t ∈ D . We average over n to obtain the mean mutual information , which can be seen as a measure of the degree of dependence between all the stocks over periods of upturns, and, respectively, downturns of the index I :
texts.text: Let M U,n and M D,n denote the mutual information of the returns of the n th stock and index, estimated from the samples { ( δS n,t , δI n,t ) } t ∈ U respectively { ( δS n,t , δI n,t ) } t ∈ D . We average over n to obtain the mean mutual information , which can be seen as a measure of the degree of dependence between all the stocks over periods of upturns, and, respectively, downturns of the index I :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: M U = 1 N N ∑ n =1 M U,n ,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: M D = 1 N N ∑ n =1 M D,n .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 4 shows the mean mutual information for varying window length - there is clearly a higher degree of dependence between the stocks returns during index downturns. However, given the hypothesis that stocks tend to 'move together' to a greater degree during index downturns, with the result that downturns are more dramatic than upturns, there is a potential problem with the measure: the mutual information between the n th stock and index is large whenever there is a high degree of dependence, not only when they tend to move in the same direction. If some stocks tend to move up when the index moves down, this would moderate the downturns, contrary to our intuition, and yet result in high values for the mean mutual information. For this reason, we also consider a correlation based measure.
texts.text: Figure 4 shows the mean mutual information for varying window length - there is clearly a higher degree of dependence between the stocks returns during index downturns. However, given the hypothesis that stocks tend to 'move together' to a greater degree during index downturns, with the result that downturns are more dramatic than upturns, there is a potential problem with the measure: the mutual information between the n th stock and index is large whenever there is a high degree of dependence, not only when they tend to move in the same direction. If some stocks tend to move up when the index moves down, this would moderate the downturns, contrary to our intuition, and yet result in high values for the mean mutual information. For this reason, we also consider a correlation based measure.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Mean correlation
texts.text: Mean correlation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let C U,n and C D,n denote the correlation between the returns of the n th stock and index, estimated from the samples { ( δS n,t , δI n,t ) } t ∈ U respectively { ( δS n,t , δI n,t ) } t ∈ D . We average over n to obtain the mean correlation , which can be seen as a measure of the degree of dependence between all the stocks over periods of upturns respectively downturns of the index I :
texts.text: Let C U,n and C D,n denote the correlation between the returns of the n th stock and index, estimated from the samples { ( δS n,t , δI n,t ) } t ∈ U respectively { ( δS n,t , δI n,t ) } t ∈ D . We average over n to obtain the mean correlation , which can be seen as a measure of the degree of dependence between all the stocks over periods of upturns respectively downturns of the index I :

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: C U = 1 N N ∑ n =1 C U,n ,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: C D = 1 N N ∑ n =1 C D,n .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 4 shows the mean correlation for varying window length - this measure of dependence between the stock returns also show markedly higher values during index downturns that during index upturns. Contrary to the mean mutual information, however, the presence of 'defensive' stocks that move up during index downturns would give negative contributions.
texts.text: Figure 4 shows the mean correlation for varying window length - this measure of dependence between the stock returns also show markedly higher values during index downturns that during index upturns. Contrary to the mean mutual information, however, the presence of 'defensive' stocks that move up during index downturns would give negative contributions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Conclusion
texts.text: Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If the gain/loss asymmetry observed for stock indices were a property of the unconditional distribution of returns, then the phenomenon should remain invariant under random permutations of the returns - this is not the case, as we have demonstrated. We may begin to rely more confidently on expectations derived from the generalized asymmetric synchronous market model, which have previously demonstrated that differences in correlated movements in index constituents for down-moves and up-moves can give rise to the kind of temporal dependence structure that produces such asymmetry. However, there are practical difficulties in exploring the correlations between the time series of the individual constituents of real stock indices, since these are not readily available, so we have shown that the gain/loss asymmetry can also be reproduced in an artificial stock index constructed as a simple average of a number of individual stocks.
texts.text: If the gain/loss asymmetry observed for stock indices were a property of the unconditional distribution of returns, then the phenomenon should remain invariant under random permutations of the returns - this is not the case, as we have demonstrated. We may begin to rely more confidently on expectations derived from the generalized asymmetric synchronous market model, which have previously demonstrated that differences in correlated movements in index constituents for down-moves and up-moves can give rise to the kind of temporal dependence structure that produces such asymmetry. However, there are practical difficulties in exploring the correlations between the time series of the individual constituents of real stock indices, since these are not readily available, so we have shown that the gain/loss asymmetry can also be reproduced in an artificial stock index constructed as a simple average of a number of individual stocks.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Considering two different measures of dependence, mean mutual information and mean correlation, we concluded that there indeed is a greater degree of dependence between the constituents of the artificial index during downturns than upturns. This part of our analysis can be seen as an attempt to overcome some of the general difficulties in formulating tractable ways of analyzing nonstationary dependence structure in multivariate stochastic processes. Future work in the direction of analyzing the dynamics of the changes in the level of dependence between asset prices would certainly be interesting - not least from the perspective of investors who seek diversification that does not break down at the worst possible time. For instance, is it possible to design a localized measure of the level of dependence between stock prices and zoom in even more on the points in time where it is changing?
texts.text: Considering two different measures of dependence, mean mutual information and mean correlation, we concluded that there indeed is a greater degree of dependence between the constituents of the artificial index during downturns than upturns. This part of our analysis can be seen as an attempt to overcome some of the general difficulties in formulating tractable ways of analyzing nonstationary dependence structure in multivariate stochastic processes. Future work in the direction of analyzing the dynamics of the changes in the level of dependence between asset prices would certainly be interesting - not least from the perspective of investors who seek diversification that does not break down at the worst possible time. For instance, is it possible to design a localized measure of the level of dependence between stock prices and zoom in even more on the points in time where it is changing?
